phrasalVerbs.definition: To stop being in one state and start being in another; to put on different clothes.
examples: I need to change into something more comfortable.
She changed into her pyjamas after dinner.
The frog changed into a prince in the fairy tale.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To take off the clothes you are wearing and put on different ones.
examples: He changed out of his work clothes as soon as he got home.
Can I change out of this dress? It's too tight.
She changed out of her uniform after school.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To fasten an item of clothing.
examples: Can you do up the buttons on my dress?
He did up his coat because it was cold.
She did up her shoelaces before running.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If two things go together, they seem good, natural, or attractive in combination.
examples: Those shoes and that bag go together perfectly.
Do you think this tie and shirt go together?
Blue and white always go together well.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make yourself look tidy and clean; to improve the appearance of something.
examples: You need to smarten up before the interview.
They smartened up the old building with fresh paint.
He smartened himself up for the wedding.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To be easy to see or notice because of being different.
examples: She always stands out in a crowd with her red hair.
His bright yellow jacket really stands out.
The new design stands out from the competition.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To use something a lot so that it no longer works or can no longer be used.
examples: I've worn out three pairs of trainers this year.
The carpet is starting to wear out in the hallway.
These jeans are completely worn out.

fillInExercises.sentence: I need to ___ ___ something warmer before we go outside.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Change into' means to put on different clothes.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ out of her wet clothes immediately.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Change out of' means to take off clothes and put on different ones.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you help me ___ ___ this zipper? It's stuck.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Do up' means to fasten something like a button or zipper.

fillInExercises.sentence: Do you think this scarf and hat ___ together?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Go together' means to look good in combination.

fillInExercises.sentence: You should ___ ___ a bit before meeting the clients.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Smarten up' means to make yourself look tidier and more presentable.

fillInExercises.sentence: Her colourful outfit really ___ out at the party.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Stand out' means to be easily noticed.

fillInExercises.sentence: These shoes are completely ___ ___. I need new ones.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Worn out' means used so much that something is no longer good.

fillInExercises.sentence: He ___ up his jacket buttons against the cold wind.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: I need to [change out of|change out of] these work clothes before the party.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Good idea. You should [change into|change into] that blue dress—it really [stands out|stand out]!

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Do you think the blue shoes [go together|go together] with it?

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Great. Can you [do up|do up] this necklace for me?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Sure! You've really [smartened up|smarten up]—you look fantastic.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I've completely [worn out|wear out] my favourite trainers.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Let's go shopping then! You need something that [stands out|stand out].

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I want something that [goes together|go together] with my new jacket.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: How about these white ones? They'd help you [smarten up|smarten up] your look.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I like them! Let me [change into|change into] them to try them on.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Good idea. Make sure you can easily [do up|do up] the laces.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Perfect fit! I'll [change out of|change out of] them and buy them.
